questions.question: Explain the winding number rule for determining interior and exterior points of a polygon. How does it handle self-intersecting polygons better than the odd-even rule?
keyPoints: Winding number counts total number of times polygon edges wind around a point
Point is interior if winding number is non-zero

questions.question: Differentiate between boundary fill and flood fill algorithms. Under what specific circumstances would you choose each algorithm?
keyPoints: Boundary fill fills region bounded by a specific boundary color
Flood fill replaces all connected pixels of a starting seed color
Boundary fill used for interactive filling with defined borders
Flood fill used for correcting color regions in image editing

questions.question: Describe the scan-line polygon filling algorithm in detail. Explain how it handles edge tables, active edge lists, and the filling process for each scan line. How does it manage holes and multiple disjoint polygons?
keyPoints: Uses edge table to store polygon edges sorted by y-coordinate
Active edge list (AEL) updated for each scan line intersection points
Fills pixels between pairs of x-intersections using parity rule
Handles holes by maintaining separate contours or using winding number

questions.question: Compare seed fill (flood fill) algorithm implementations using stack-based (recursive) versus queue-based (iterative) approaches. Discuss their efficiency, memory requirements, and suitability for different polygon complexities including those with holes.
keyPoints: Recursive uses system stack (limited depth, stack overflow risk)
Iterative uses explicit queue (better memory control, handles large areas)
4-connected fills smaller regions vs 8-connected for diagonal connectivity
Boundary fill handles polygons with holes by maintaining border integrity
